result.diagram_code:
sequenceDiagram
    participant I as Attacker
    participant B as Responder xB=B
    participant S as Server

    I->>B: start responder with xB = B
    I->>B: {y, B}_pkB
    Note over B: decrypts message 3 as (NY=y, hostY=B)\nbeginAparam(B,B)
    B->>S: (B, B)
    S->>B: sign((pkB, B), skS)
    Note over B: accepts certificate for hostY = B
    B->>I: {y, Nb, B}_pkB
    I->>B: {y}_pkB
    Note over B: buggy check compares decrypted message 7 with NY=y\ninstead of Nb
    Note over B: endBparam(B,B), endBfull(B,B,pkB,pkB,y,Nb)
    B->>I: sencrypt(secretBNa, y)
    Note over I: since y was attacker-chosen, attacker decrypts and learns secretBNa
result.explanation: The responder accepts message 7 if it decrypts to NY (the nonce received in message 3) instead of Nb (the fresh challenge it generated). In the trace, the attacker chooses NY=y, sends {y,B}_pkB, obtains B's reply {y,Nb,B}_pkB, and then returns {y}_pkB. Because pkB is public, this forged final message passes the buggy test, so B executes endBparam/endBfull without any matching initiator run and leaks secretBNa encrypted under attacker-known y.
result.patch_suggestion:
Fix the responder's final check to validate the responder's fresh nonce and use the role-dependent secret key:
replace
if nonce_to_bitstring(NY) = decrypt(m3, skB) then
with
if nonce_to_bitstring(Nb) = decrypt(m3, skxB) then
result.notes: The provided trace shows the attack on the B role specifically (xB=B, hostY=B). The same line also uses skB instead of skxB; that is not needed for this concrete trace, but correcting it is the right minimal code change at the vulnerable check.
